animals_wildlife.quizQuestion: How much blood can a leech consume relative to its body weight?
animals_wildlife.correctAnswer: Up to 10 times its body weight
animals_wildlife.explanation: Leeches are extraordinary feeders. A single blood meal can be up to 10 times the leech's own unfed body weight, and digestion is so slow that the leech may not need to feed again for 6 months or more.

animals_wildlife.quizQuestion: How many eyelids does a camel have?
animals_wildlife.explanation: The third eyelid is a translucent nictitating membrane that slides horizontally across the eye to clear dust while still allowing the camel to see. The double row of eyelashes also acts as a sand filter.

animals_wildlife.quizQuestion: How often do Canada lynx and snowshoe hare populations peak in their famous cycle?
animals_wildlife.explanation: The Canada lynx and snowshoe hare relationship is one of ecology's most studied predator-prey cycles. As hare populations boom, lynx populations follow; as hares crash from over-grazing, lynx starve and their numbers plummet — cycling roughly every 9-10 years.

art_architecture.quizQuestion: Who published the Surrealist Manifesto that launched the movement in 1924?
art_architecture.explanation: André Breton (1896–1966) published the Manifeste du surréalisme in October 1924 in Paris. Drawing on Freud's psychoanalytic theories, he defined Surrealism as 'pure psychic automatism' — creating art from the unfiltered unconscious mind, bypassing rational thought.

art_architecture.quizQuestion: How long did photographer Iain Macmillan have to shoot the Abbey Road album cover?
art_architecture.explanation: Abbey Road was recorded at EMI Studios on Abbey Road, St. John's Wood, London. The photo shoot took place on the morning of 8 August 1969. Paul McCartney is barefoot in the image, which spawned the famous 'Paul is dead' conspiracy theory. The crossing itself was given Grade II listed building status by Historic England in 2010, recognising its cultural significance.

art_architecture.quizQuestion: From which film is Monroe's famous white dress subway grate scene?
art_architecture.explanation: The scene of Monroe's white halter dress billowing over a subway grate was filmed on Lexington Avenue in New York in 1954 to publicity crowds of 2,000. Directed by Billy Wilder, the image became the defining icon of Hollywood glamour in commercial and pop art.

food_cuisine.quizQuestion: What is most sushi restaurant 'wasabi' actually made from?
food_cuisine.explanation: True wasabi (Wasabia japonica) is expensive and rare—it grows only in cold, running water streams and is notoriously difficult to cultivate. Most restaurants outside Japan (and many inside) use horseradish paste dyed green with food coloring as a much cheaper substitute.

food_cuisine.quizQuestion: What percentage of rice is milled away for daiginjo sake?
food_cuisine.explanation: Daiginjo ('grand premium') sake requires the rice to be milled to 50% of its original size or smaller, removing the outer protein and oil layers that can add harsher flavors, leaving only the pure starch core for fermentation.

food_cuisine.quizQuestion: What determines whether miso is white or dark red?
food_cuisine.explanation: White (shiro) miso is fermented for days to weeks, producing a mild, sweet flavor. Red (aka) miso ferments for months to years, developing intense umami and saltiness. The Maillard reaction during long fermentation deepens the color and complexity.

general_knowledge.quizQuestion: What was the first device that could record and play back sound?
general_knowledge.explanation: Edison invented the phonograph in 1877. It recorded sound by engraving a groove onto a rotating tinfoil cylinder. The first words ever recorded and played back were 'Mary had a little lamb.'

general_knowledge.quizQuestion: Which emperor ordered the initial construction of the Great Wall?
general_knowledge.explanation: Qin Shi Huang, China's first emperor, ordered the unification of earlier northern fortifications into a single wall around 221 BCE. Hundreds of thousands of workers, including soldiers and peasants, built it under brutal conditions.

general_knowledge.quizQuestion: In what year was Wikipedia founded?
general_knowledge.explanation: Wikipedia launched on January 15, 2001, as a complement to the expert-written Nupedia. Within a year it had 20,000 articles. Today it has over 60 million articles across 300+ languages, maintained by volunteer editors.

geography.quizQuestion: Which group of animals dominates New Zealand's native wildlife niche that would normally be filled by land mammals?
geography.explanation: With no predatory land mammals for millions of years, New Zealand's birds evolved to fill every ecological niche. Many became flightless — including the kiwi, kakapo (world's heaviest parrot), and the extinct moa (up to 3.6 m tall). When humans and rats arrived, these birds had no fear of ground predators and were devastated.

geography.quizQuestion: Which African country lies near 0° latitude AND 0° longitude?
geography.explanation: The intersection of the equator and Prime Meridian (the 'null point' at 0°N, 0°E) lies in the Gulf of Guinea south of Ghana. Ghana is the country closest to this exact geographic center of Earth's coordinate system.

geography.quizQuestion: Which country is the largest in Africa by land area?
geography.explanation: Algeria covers approximately 2.38 million square kilometers, making it the largest country in Africa (since South Sudan's independence moved Sudan to second place) and the largest in the Arab world. Despite its enormous size, about 90% of Algerians live on the northern 12% of the country — the fertile Mediterranean coast. The vast majority of Algerian territory is covered by the Sahara Desert.

history.quizQuestion: What did Epicurus's famous argument claim about the relationship between life and death?
history.explanation: Epicurus argued that fear of death is irrational because when you are alive, death is absent, and when death comes, you no longer exist to experience it. Therefore, death can never harm you — you will never be there to suffer it. This elegant logical argument has comforted people for 2,300 years.

history.quizQuestion: From which country's noodle traditions did ramen most likely evolve?
history.explanation: Ramen only became a distinctly 'Japanese' food in the mid-1900s — before that it was considered a foreign Chinese dish sold by street vendors!

history.quizQuestion: Which dynasty did Saladin found that ruled Egypt and Syria?
history.explanation: Saladin founded the Ayyubid dynasty after overthrowing the Fatimid caliphate in Egypt in 1171. The dynasty unified Egypt, Syria, and parts of Mesopotamia under a single Muslim ruler for the first time in generations. The Ayyubids were known for patronizing scholarship, architecture, and trade across the medieval Islamic world.

human_body_health.quizQuestion: Which DNA base always pairs with adenine (A)?
human_body_health.explanation: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) consists of two strands wound in a double helix. Each strand is made of nucleotides containing one of four bases: Adenine (A), Thymine (T), Guanine (G), and Cytosine (C). Base pairing is specific and complementary: A always pairs with T (connected by 2 hydrogen bonds) and G always pairs with C (3 hydrogen bonds). This complementarity is the basis for DNA replication and transcription.

human_body_health.quizQuestion: Hippocrates proposed that health depends on balancing how many humors?
human_body_health.explanation: The four humors (blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile) were thought to correspond to personality types: sanguine, phlegmatic, choleric, and melancholic. This theory dominated medicine for 1,500 years.

human_body_health.quizQuestion: What Latin phrase summarizes the Hippocratic principle of non-maleficence?
human_body_health.explanation: 'Primum non nocere' — 'first, do no harm' — is a foundational principle of medical ethics. Ironically, this exact phrase doesn't appear in the original Hippocratic Oath but emerged in the 19th century.

mythology_folklore.quizQuestion: What sign foretold Ambrose's future eloquence, according to legend?
mythology_folklore.correctAnswer: A swarm of bees settled harmlessly on his face as an infant
distractors: A dove landed on his shoulder at baptism
He spoke in full sentences at birth
Lightning struck a nearby tree without harming him
mythology_folklore.explanation: The bee legend is a recurring symbol of eloquence in medieval hagiography. Honey symbolized sweet speech; the sting, sharp wit.

mythology_folklore.quizQuestion: Which Egyptian sacred bird is considered the direct precursor to the phoenix myth?
mythology_folklore.explanation: The Egyptian bennu was a self-created deity in the form of a heron that perched atop the primordial mound at creation. It was associated with Ra and Osiris, particularly with resurrection and the rising sun. When Greek historians visited Egypt, they merged the bennu tradition with Greek cosmology, producing the phoenix as Europeans came to know it. Heliopolis (City of the Sun) was said to be where the phoenix brought its father's ashes each cycle.

mythology_folklore.quizQuestion: Which deity is Adi Shankara traditionally considered an avatar of?
mythology_folklore.explanation: Adi Shankara is widely regarded by his followers as a partial avatar (amsha-avatara) of Shiva, specifically sent to counteract the spread of Buddhism and Mimamsa ritualism and to restore the non-dualist (Advaita) interpretation of the Upanishads. Several traditional biographies, particularly the Madhaviya Shankara Digvijaya, describe his divine origin and miraculous birth. His philosophical project — establishing the identity of the individual self (atman) with ultimate reality (Brahman) — is often framed as Shiva's own teaching.

natural_sciences.quizQuestion: In what year did Newton publish the Principia Mathematica?
natural_sciences.explanation: Newton's Principia (1687) defined the three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation. It unified terrestrial and celestial mechanics under a single mathematical framework, transforming physics and launching the scientific revolution's peak.

natural_sciences.quizQuestion: What is the term for a volcano capable of an eruption exceeding 1,000 cubic km?
natural_sciences.explanation: Supervolcanoes, such as those at Yellowstone and Lake Toba, can produce eruptions thousands of times larger than ordinary volcanoes. The Toba eruption ~74,000 years ago may have caused a global volcanic winter that nearly wiped out human populations.

natural_sciences.quizQuestion: What unusual process causes peanut pods to develop underground?
natural_sciences.explanation: After a peanut flower is fertilized above ground, a peg (gynophore) grows downward and buries the developing pod 2-7 cm underground. There the pod matures into what we eat. This is called geocarpy and is rare in plants.

space_astronomy.quizQuestion: What makes Venus's rotation unusual compared to most planets?
space_astronomy.correctAnswer: It rotates in the opposite (retrograde) direction
distractors: It rotates faster than any other planet
It does not rotate at all
It rotates in the same direction as the Sun
It spins due to magnetic forces
Its rotation matches its orbital period exactly
space_astronomy.explanation: Venus rotates from east to west (retrograde), the opposite direction of Earth and most other planets. This means the Sun rises in the west and sets in the east on Venus. Its rotation is also extremely slow — a Venusian day is longer than its year.

space_astronomy.quizQuestion: What astronomical model did Copernicus propose?
space_astronomy.explanation: In 1543, Copernicus published 'De Revolutionibus,' proposing the Sun is the center of the solar system. This replaced the ancient geocentric (Earth-centered) model dominant since Ptolemy.

space_astronomy.quizQuestion: What did Hubble prove in 1923 by observing Cepheid stars in Andromeda?
space_astronomy.correctAnswer: Andromeda is a separate galaxy beyond the Milky Way
distractors: Andromeda is part of the Milky Way
Cepheid stars are the largest in the universe
The Milky Way is the only galaxy
Stars form from nebulae within our galaxy only
space_astronomy.explanation: Before Hubble, many astronomers believed all nebulae were inside the Milky Way. His Andromeda measurements settled the 'Great Debate' about the scale of the universe.

vocab_cs.correctAnswer: a male given name, equivalent to English...
vocab_cs.explanation: Jiří — a male given name, equivalent to English George. Part of speech: noun.
šálek — cup (vessel for drinking). Part of speech: noun.
napsat — to write. Part of speech: verb.

vocab_de.explanation: kommunistisch — communist, communistic. Part of speech: adjective.
privat — private. Part of speech: adjective.
Elektroingenieur — electrical engineer. Part of speech: noun.

vocab_es.explanation: encima — on top. Part of speech: adverb. Also: on oneself, over oneself, additionally, nevertheless, still, to top it off, on top of that
energía — energy. Part of speech: noun.
yacer — to lie; to recline. Part of speech: verb. Also: to have sex, to fuck

vocab_fr.explanation: pipi — wee, wee-wee, pee. Part of speech: noun.
vaste — vast. Part of speech: adjective.
éthique — ethical. Part of speech: adjective.

vocab_ja.quizQuestion: What does the grammar pattern 'について' mean?
vocab_ja.explanation: について — regarding, about. Example: 私が子供の頃、日本という国について知っていたことは、東アジアの島国だということぐらいでした。（here ぐらい expresses the aproximation) (In my childhood, all I knew about Japan was that it is an insular country situated in Eastern Asia.)
vocab_ja.exampleSentence: 私が子供の頃、日本という国について知っていたことは、東アジアの島国だということぐらいでした。（here ぐらい expresses the aproximation) (In my childhood, all I knew about Japan was that it is an insular country situated in Eastern Asia.)

vocab_ko.quizQuestion: Which Korean grammar pattern means 'Indicates that the second action happens'?
vocab_ko.explanation: Formation: Verb stem + 하자 | Example: 문을 열자마자 강아지가 들어왔어요. (As soon as I opened the door, the dog came in.)
상주 — The state of staying at one place continuously. Part of speech: noun.
아흐레 — Nine days. Part of speech: noun. Also: The ninth day when counting from the first day of the month

vocab_nl.explanation: omwille — only used in omwille van. Part of speech: adverb.
authenticiteit — authenticity. Part of speech: noun.
aanwezigheid — presence. Part of speech: noun. Also: attendance

vocab_zh.explanation: 黑心 (hēi xīn) — ruthless and lacking in conscience. Part of speech: n. Also: vicious mind full of hatred and jealousy, black core (flaw in pottery)
保健 (bǎo jiàn) — health protection. Part of speech: n. Also: health care, to maintain in good health
物证 (wù zhèng) — material evidence. Part of speech: n.
